questions.question: Let f : A -> B be an injective (one-to-one) function. Define g : 2^A -> 2^B as: g(C) = {f(x) | x in C}, for all subsets C of A. Define h : 2^B -> 2^A as: h(D) = {x | x in A, f(x) in D}, for all subsets D of B. Which of the following statements is always true? A. g(h(D)) subseteq D  B. g(h(D)) supseteq D  C. g(h(D)) intersect D = phi  D. g(h(D)) intersect (B - D) != phi
questions.to_find: Which containment between g(h(D)) and D always holds

understand.plain: Here g is the 'image' map (push a subset of A forward through f) and h is the 'preimage' map (pull a subset of B back through f). Starting from a subset D of B, we first take its preimage h(D) = all points of A that f sends into D, then push that forward with g. Every element we get back was, by construction, a value f(x) with x already chosen because f(x) is in D, so the result can never leave D. Hence g(h(D)) sits inside D.

keywords.term: Image of a set, g(C) = f[C]
keywords.explain: The image collects the outputs f(x) for every x in the input subset C; it is a subset of the codomain B.
keywords.example: If f(1)=a, f(2)=b and C={1,2}, then g(C)={a,b}.

keywords.term: Preimage of a set, h(D) = f^{-1}[D]
keywords.explain: The preimage collects every x in A whose output f(x) falls inside D; it is a subset of the domain A.
keywords.example: If f(1)=a, f(2)=b and D={a}, then h(D)={1} because only f(1) lands in D.

keywords.term: Image of a preimage: f[f^{-1}[D]] subseteq D
keywords.explain: Pushing the preimage of D forward can only recover points of D that are actually hit by f; you never gain points outside D, so the result is contained in D.
keywords.example: With f(1)=a, D={a,c}: h(D)={1}, g(h(D))={a} subseteq {a,c}=D.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='558' height='238' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='30' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='430' y='30' class='lbl ink'>B</text><circle cx='80' cy='90' r='14' class='hi'/><text x='74' y='95' class='sub ink'>x1</text><circle cx='80' cy='150' r='14' class='hi'/><text x='74' y='155' class='sub ink'>x2</text><rect x='400' y='60' width='120' height='150' class='bg'/><text x='415' y='80' class='sub mut'>D (subset of B)</text><circle cx='460' cy='110' r='14' class='ok'/><text x='452' y='115' class='sub ink'>d1</text><circle cx='460' cy='170' r='14' class='ok'/><text x='452' y='175' class='sub ink'>d2</text><line x1='94' y1='90' x2='446' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='94' y1='150' x2='446' y2='170' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='150' y='205' class='tag ink'>h(D) = preimage in A; g(h(D)) lands back inside D</text><text x='150' y='228' class='tag ok'>g(h(D)) subseteq D</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Points of A whose images lie in D are pulled back by h, then pushed forward by g; the arrows land only inside D, so g(h(D)) is contained in D.

given.aim: Determine the always-true relationship between g(h(D)) and the subset D of B.

terms.meaning: A one-to-one function: distinct inputs have distinct outputs, so no two elements of A share an image
terms.connects: Injectivity gives g(h(D)) = D intersect f[A], strengthening the containment to equality on hit points

terms.meaning: The forward image map sending a subset of A to its set of images in B
terms.connects: Applied last, to h(D), producing g(h(D))

terms.term: h(D) = {x in A : f(x) in D}
terms.meaning: The backward (preimage) map sending a subset of B to the inputs that map into it
terms.connects: Computed first; its image under g is the quantity in question

terms.meaning: An arbitrary subset of the codomain, the starting set
terms.example: D = {a, c} where c may not be an image of any x
terms.connects: g(h(D)) recovers only the part of D actually reached by f, so it stays inside D

given.plan: Trace an arbitrary element of g(h(D)) back to its source and show it must lie in D; this proves g(h(D)) subseteq D, then rule out the other options with the same trace.

steps.title: Unfold the definitions of g and h
steps.apply: By definition h(D) = {x in A : f(x) in D}. Take any element y in g(h(D)); then y = f(x) for some x in h(D), and x in h(D) means f(x) in D. So y = f(x) is in D. Since every element of g(h(D)) lies in D, we get g(h(D)) subseteq D.
steps.note: This containment holds for ANY function; injectivity is not even needed for the subseteq direction.

steps.title: Reject the other options
steps.apply: B (g(h(D)) supseteq D) fails: if D contains an element b in B that is not an image of any x (b not in f[A]), then b is never recovered, so D is not contained in g(h(D)). C (g(h(D)) intersect D = phi) fails: pick any b = f(x) in D, then b in g(h(D)) and b in D, so the intersection is non-empty. D (g(h(D)) intersect (B - D) != phi) fails: by Step 1, g(h(D)) subseteq D, so it is disjoint from B - D, making the intersection empty. Only A survives.
steps.note: Choosing a D with an element outside the range f[A] is the clean counterexample that kills option B.

solution.result: g(h(D)) subseteq D always holds, so the answer is Option A.

questions.formula_note: The key identity is f[f^{-1}[D]] subseteq D: pushing the preimage of D forward can only land back inside D, which holds for every function and immediately gives option A.

questions.question: Let Sigma = {a, b, c, d, e} be an alphabet. We define an encoding scheme as follows: g(a) = 3, g(b) = 5, g(c) = 7, g(d) = 9, g(e) = 11. Let p_i denote the i-th prime number (p_1 = 2). For a non-empty string s = a_1 ... a_n, where each a_i in Sigma, define f(s) = prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{g(a_i)}. For a non-empty sequence (s_1, ..., s_n) of strings from Sigma^+, define h((s_1, ..., s_n)) = prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{f(s_i)}. Which of the following numbers is the encoding, h, of a non-empty sequence of strings? A. 2^7 * 3^7 * 5^7  B. 2^8 * 3^8 * 5^8  C. 2^9 * 3^9 * 5^9  D. 2^10 * 3^10 * 5^10
questions.to_find: Which prime-power number is a valid sequence encoding h

understand.plain: The scheme uses unique prime factorization twice. A string s becomes f(s) = a product of primes 2, 3, 5, ... whose exponents are the g-values 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 (all ODD). A sequence becomes h = product of primes 2, 3, 5, ... whose exponents are the f-values of the strings. So for a given number to be a valid h, each of its prime exponents must itself be a legal f(s). The smallest legal f-value is f of a one-letter string a, namely 2^3 = 8. So an exponent of 8 is achievable, but 7, 9, 10 are not f-values, which selects option B.

keywords.explain: Every positive integer factors uniquely into primes, so a number like 2^e1 * 3^e2 * 5^e3 encodes the exponent list (e1, e2, e3) without ambiguity.

keywords.term: What counts as a valid f(s)
keywords.explain: f(s) = prod p_i^{g(a_i)} where each g-value is odd (3,5,7,9,11). The single-letter string a gives the minimal f-value 2^3 = 8; longer strings or other letters give larger values.
For h to be a real sequence encoding, read off each prime's exponent and verify that exponent is some f(s_i); if any exponent is not a possible f-value, the number is not a valid h.
keywords.example: Exponent 8 = f('a') is fine; exponent 7 cannot equal any f(s) since 7 is not of the form 2^(odd).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='558' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='34' class='lbl ink'>h = 2^(f(s1)) * 3^(f(s2)) * 5^(f(s3))</text><rect x='20' y='56' width='250' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='35' y='84' class='sub ink'>need exponent = some f(s)</text><text x='35' y='106' class='sub ink'>min f = f('a') = 2^3 = 8</text><rect x='300' y='56' width='240' height='150' class='bg'/><text x='315' y='80' class='sub mut'>exponent test</text><text x='315' y='108' class='sub no'>7 = f(s)?  NO</text><text x='315' y='134' class='sub ok'>8 = 2^3 = f('a')?  YES</text><text x='315' y='160' class='sub no'>9 = f(s)?  NO</text><text x='315' y='186' class='sub no'>10 = f(s)?  NO</text><text x='20' y='150' class='tag ok'>Only 8 is a valid f-value</text><text x='20' y='178' class='big ok'>so 2^8*3^8*5^8 (B)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A test table showing only exponent 8 equals a legal f-value (2^3 = f('a')), while 7, 9 and 10 are not f-values, so only 2^8*3^8*5^8 is a valid encoding.

given.aim: Find the number whose prime exponents are all legitimate f(s) values, hence a real sequence encoding.

terms.meaning: Letter-to-number map; all five values are odd, used as exponents inside f
terms.example: g(a) = 3 is the smallest, giving the smallest single-letter exponent
terms.connects: Determines which exponents an f-value can carry, hence the minimal f = 2^3 = 8

terms.meaning: String encoding: i-th letter contributes prime p_i raised to its g-value
terms.connects: Each prime exponent of h must equal some f(s_i)

terms.meaning: Sequence encoding: i-th string contributes prime p_i raised to its f-value
terms.connects: We test each option as a candidate h

terms.meaning: The first three primes, the only bases appearing in all four options
terms.example: An option 2^x * 3^y * 5^z encodes a 3-string sequence with f-values x, y, z
terms.connects: Forces each exponent to be a valid f-value for the number to be a real h

given.plan: Compute the minimum possible f-value (single letter a gives 2^3 = 8), then check each option's exponents: a number is a valid h only when every exponent equals some achievable f(s).

steps.title: Find the smallest legal f-value
steps.apply: The single-letter string 'a' has f('a') = 2^{g(a)} = 2^3 = 8. Any string has at least one letter, so its f-value is at least 2^3 = 8, and every f-value is a product of primes raised to ODD exponents (the g-values 3,5,7,9,11). Thus 8 is the minimum f-value and is achievable.
steps.note: Because g-values are odd, an f-value can never be 2^(even) like 4; the smallest is 2^3 = 8.

steps.title: Test each option's exponents against valid f-values
steps.apply: Each option is 2^k * 3^k * 5^k, so we just need k to be a valid f(s). A: k = 7 is below 8 and is not of the form 2^(odd) * ..., so 7 is not an f-value -> invalid. B: k = 8 = 2^3 = f('a'), a genuine f-value -> the sequence (a, a, a) encodes to 2^8 * 3^8 * 5^8 -> VALID. C: k = 9 is not an f-value (f-values that are pure powers of 2 are 2^3=8, 2^5=32, ...; 9 is not among them) -> invalid. D: k = 10 is even, but an f-value that is a pure power of 2 must be 2^(odd); 10 is not, and 10 is not any other f-value -> invalid.
steps.note: All options share the same exponent on 2, 3, 5, so the whole question reduces to: is that single exponent a legal f-value?

solution.result: Only 2^8 * 3^8 * 5^8 has exponents equal to a valid f-value (8 = f('a')), so the answer is Option B.

questions.formula_note: Unique prime factorization makes h decodable: each prime exponent must itself be a valid f(s), and since the minimal f-value is 2^3 = 8 (string 'a'), only exponent 8 qualifies, giving option B.

questions.question: Let f : B -> C and g : A -> B be two functions and let h = f o g. Given that h is an onto function, which one of the following is TRUE? A. f and g should both be onto functions  B. f should be onto but g need not be onto  C. g should be onto but f need not be onto  D. both f and g need not be onto
questions.answer: B. f should be onto but g need not be onto
questions.to_find: What onto-ness of h = f o g forces about f and g

understand.plain: Composition h = f o g first applies g (from A to B) and then f (from B to C). If h hits every element of C, then in particular the final map f must reach every element of C, because the only way an output ever lands in C is through f. So f is forced to be onto. But g need not be onto: g can miss parts of B as long as f can still cover all of C from the part of B that g does reach.

keywords.explain: A function is onto if every element of its codomain is an output of some input; nothing in the codomain is left unhit.
keywords.example: f : {1,2} -> {x} with f(1)=f(2)=x is onto since x is reached.

keywords.explain: (f o g)(a) = f(g(a)); you feed a into g, then feed g(a) into f. Its range is a subset of the range of f.
keywords.example: If g(a)=b and f(b)=c then (f o g)(a)=c.

keywords.term: Surjectivity propagates to the outer map
keywords.explain: If a composition f o g is onto, the outer function f must be onto, since range(f o g) subseteq range(f) subseteq C and range(f o g) = C forces range(f) = C.
keywords.example: h onto C => f onto C; the inner g may still skip elements of B.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='598' height='228' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='30' class='lbl ink'>A</text><text x='270' y='30' class='lbl ink'>B</text><text x='510' y='30' class='lbl ink'>C</text><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='60' cy='150' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='290' cy='70' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='290' cy='130' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='290' cy='190' r='12' class='no'/><text x='305' y='195' class='sub mut'>g misses this</text><circle cx='520' cy='90' r='12' class='ok'/><circle cx='520' cy='150' r='12' class='ok'/><line x1='72' y1='90' x2='278' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='72' y1='150' x2='278' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='302' y1='70' x2='508' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='302' y1='130' x2='508' y2='150' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='40' y='220' class='tag ink'>h = f o g onto C  =>  f onto (every C reached), g need not be onto</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: g maps A into B but skips one element of B; f then covers all of C, so the composition is onto C even though g is not onto.

given.aim: State the precise surjectivity that h = f o g being onto forces on f and g.

terms.meaning: The inner (first-applied) function from A to B
terms.example: g may hit only part of B
terms.connects: Its range feeds into f; it need not cover all of B

terms.meaning: The outer (last-applied) function from B to C
terms.example: f must reach every element of C
terms.connects: Range of the composition is contained in range of f, forcing f onto

terms.term: h = f o g : A -> C
terms.meaning: The composition; h(a) = f(g(a))
terms.example: h onto means every c in C equals f(g(a)) for some a
terms.connects: Given onto, it pins down f as onto but leaves g free

terms.meaning: Codomain fully covered: every target element is an output
terms.example: f onto C: for each c in C there is b in B with f(b)=c
terms.connects: The property being propagated from h to f

given.plan: Use range(f o g) subseteq range(f) subseteq C; if h is onto then range(f) = C so f is onto, and exhibit an example where g is not onto yet h still is, ruling out the stronger options.

steps.title: Onto composition forces f onto
steps.formula_raw: range(f o g) subseteq range(f) subseteq C ;  h onto => range(f) = C
steps.apply: Every output of h equals f(g(a)), which is a value of f, so range(h) subseteq range(f). Since h is onto, range(h) = C, giving C subseteq range(f) subseteq C, i.e. range(f) = C. Therefore f must be onto.
steps.note: The outer function is the last thing applied, so it alone determines what can appear in C.

steps.title: Show g need not be onto
steps.apply: Take A = {1}, B = {p, q}, C = {x}. Let g(1) = p (so g misses q, g is NOT onto) and f(p) = f(q) = x (f is onto C). Then h(1) = f(g(1)) = f(p) = x, so h is onto C. Here h is onto and f is onto, yet g is not onto. Hence onto-ness of g is not required.
steps.note: This single example eliminates A (needs g onto) and C (claims f need not be onto), and beats D (claims f need not be onto).

solution.result: f must be onto while g need not be onto, so the answer is Option B.

questions.formula_note: Since range(f o g) subseteq range(f), an onto composition forces the outer function f to be onto, while the inner g can skip elements of B, pinning the answer to option B.

questions.question: Let X, Y, Z be sets of sizes x, y and z respectively. Let W = X x Y and E be the set of all subsets of W. The number of functions from Z to E is A. z^(2^(xy))  B. z * 2^(xy)  C. z^(2^(x+y))  D. 2^(xyz)
questions.to_find: The number of functions from Z to the power set of X x Y

understand.plain: Build the answer in layers. The Cartesian product W = X x Y has x*y ordered pairs. E is the power set of W, so it has 2^(xy) elements. The number of functions from a set of size z (that is Z) to a set of size 2^(xy) (that is E) is (size of E) raised to (size of Z) = (2^(xy))^z. Using the exponent rule (2^(xy))^z = 2^(xyz), which is option D.

keywords.explain: |X x Y| = |X| * |Y|; each ordered pair pairs one element of X with one of Y.
A set with m elements has exactly 2^m subsets, so |power set of W| = 2^(|W|).
The number of functions from a set of size m to a set of size n is n^m: each of the m inputs independently chooses one of n outputs.
keywords.example: Functions from a 2-element set to a 3-element set: 3^2 = 9.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='558' height='228' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='30' width='150' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='32' y='60' class='sub ink'>W = X x Y</text><text x='32' y='100' class='sub mut'>|W| = x*y</text><rect x='205' y='30' width='150' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='217' y='60' class='sub ink'>E = P(W)</text><text x='217' y='100' class='sub mut'>|E| = 2^(xy)</text><rect x='390' y='30' width='150' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='402' y='60' class='sub ink'>Z -> E</text><text x='402' y='100' class='sub mut'>|E|^|Z| = (2^(xy))^z</text><text x='100' y='55' class='lbl ink'></text><text x='178' y='62' class='lbl mut'>-></text><text x='363' y='62' class='lbl mut'>-></text><text x='20' y='160' class='lbl ink'>(2^(xy))^z = 2^(xy*z) = 2^(xyz)</text><text x='20' y='200' class='big ok'>Answer: 2^(xyz)  (D)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three stages: W has x*y pairs, its power set E has 2^(xy) elements, and functions from Z to E number (2^(xy))^z = 2^(xyz).

given.aim: Count all functions from Z into E = power set of (X x Y).

terms.term: X, Y, Z with sizes x, y, z
terms.meaning: Three finite sets whose cardinalities feed the final count
terms.connects: x and y build W; z is the domain size of the functions

terms.meaning: The Cartesian product, set of all ordered pairs (a, b) with a in X, b in Y
terms.connects: Its size determines |E| = 2^(xy)

terms.meaning: The set of all subsets of W; it is the codomain of the functions
terms.connects: Serves as the n in the n^m function count

terms.meaning: Each of the z elements of Z is assigned one of the |E| subsets
terms.connects: Exponent rule collapses this to 2^(xyz)

given.plan: Compute |W| = xy, then |E| = 2^(xy), then count functions as |E|^z = (2^(xy))^z, and simplify with the power-of-a-power rule to 2^(xyz).

steps.title: Size of W and of its power set E
steps.apply: W = X x Y has |X| * |Y| = x*y ordered pairs. E is the set of all subsets of W, so by the subset-count rule |E| = 2^(xy).
steps.note: The power set of an m-element set always has 2^m members.

steps.title: Count functions from Z to E
steps.apply: The number of functions from a z-element set to an |E|-element set is |E|^z = (2^(xy))^z. By the power-of-a-power rule, (2^(xy))^z = 2^(xy*z) = 2^(xyz).
steps.note: Each of the z inputs independently picks one of the 2^(xy) subsets, giving the exponential count.

solution.result: The number of functions from Z to E is 2^(xyz), so the answer is Option D.

questions.formula_note: Stacking |W| = xy, |E| = 2^(xy), then (2^(xy))^z = 2^(xyz) via the function-count n^m and the power-of-a-power rule gives option D.

questions.question: Let S = {1, 2, 3, ..., m}, m > 3. Let X_1, ..., X_n be subsets of S each of size 3. Define a function f from S to the set of natural numbers as, f(i) is the number of sets X_j that contain the element i. That is f(i) = |{j | i in X_j}|, then sum_{i=1}^{m} f(i) is: A. 3m  B. 3n  C. 2m + 1  D. 2n + 1
questions.to_find: The value of sum over all elements of S of f(i)

understand.plain: f(i) counts how many of the n subsets contain element i. Summing f(i) over all m elements counts, in total, every (element, set) membership pair across all the subsets. But we can count the same pairs the other way: each of the n subsets has exactly 3 elements, so it contributes 3 memberships. Adding over the n subsets gives 3n. This double counting trick makes the sum 3n regardless of m.

keywords.explain: Count the same collection of objects two different ways; the two totals must be equal, which yields an identity.
keywords.example: Counting (element, set) pairs by element gives sum f(i); by set gives sum |X_j|.

keywords.explain: The total number of element-in-set incidences equals both the sum of element multiplicities and the sum of set sizes.
Each X_j has exactly 3 elements, so |X_j| = 3 for every j and the right-hand sum is just 3 added n times.
keywords.example: n = 4 sets each of size 3 contribute 4 * 3 = 12 memberships.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='558' height='238' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Count (element, set) memberships two ways</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='250' height='150' class='hi'/><text x='32' y='76' class='sub ink'>By element i:</text><text x='32' y='102' class='sub ink'>sum_{i=1..m} f(i)</text><text x='32' y='150' class='sub mut'>f(i) = # sets containing i</text><rect x='300' y='50' width='240' height='150' class='hi'/><text x='312' y='76' class='sub ink'>By set X_j:</text><text x='312' y='102' class='sub ink'>sum_{j=1..n} |X_j|</text><text x='312' y='150' class='sub mut'>each |X_j| = 3</text><text x='312' y='178' class='sub ok'>= 3 + 3 + ... (n times) = 3n</text><text x='20' y='228' class='big ok'>sum f(i) = 3n  (B)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Both columns count the same element-set memberships; summing by element gives sum f(i), summing by set gives n sets times size 3 = 3n.

given.aim: Evaluate sum_{i=1}^{m} f(i) where f(i) counts the subsets containing element i.

terms.meaning: The ground set with m elements (m > 3)
terms.example: Element i ranges over 1 to m in the outer sum
terms.connects: The sum runs over all elements of S

terms.meaning: n subsets of S, every one having exactly 3 elements
terms.connects: Each contributes exactly 3 to the total membership count

terms.meaning: The number of subsets that contain the element i
terms.example: If element 2 appears in X_1 and X_3 only, f(2) = 2
terms.connects: Summed over i, it totals all memberships

terms.meaning: The number of (element, set) pairs with element in set
terms.connects: Equals 3n because each of the n sets has size 3

given.plan: Equate the two ways of counting (element, set) memberships: sum_i f(i) equals sum_j |X_j|, and since every |X_j| = 3 there are n of them, the total is 3n.

steps.title: Rewrite the sum as a count of memberships
steps.formula_raw: sum_{i=1}^{m} f(i) = sum_{i=1}^{m} |{j : i in X_j}| = (number of (i, j) pairs with i in X_j)
steps.apply: f(i) counts the sets containing i, so summing over all elements i counts every incidence (i, X_j) with i in X_j exactly once. This is the total number of element-set memberships across all n subsets.
steps.note: This reorganizes the sum from 'per element' to 'all memberships', the heart of double counting.

steps.title: Count the same memberships by set
steps.apply: The same incidences can be counted by set: each subset X_j contributes |X_j| memberships, and every X_j has size 3. Summing over the n subsets gives 3 + 3 + ... + 3 = 3n. Therefore sum_{i=1}^{m} f(i) = 3n.
steps.note: The answer depends on n (number of subsets), not on m; options with m are distractors.

solution.result: sum_{i=1}^{m} f(i) = 3n, so the answer is Option B.

questions.formula_note: By double counting, sum_i f(i) equals the total element-set memberships, which is also sum_j |X_j| = 3n since each of the n subsets has size 3; this gives option B and shows the answer is independent of m.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of different Boolean functions involving n Boolean variables? A. n^2  B. 2^n  C. 2^(2^n)  D. 2^(n^2)
questions.to_find: The number of distinct Boolean functions of n variables

understand.plain: A Boolean function of n variables is fixed once you decide its output (0 or 1) for every input combination. With n variables there are 2^n possible input rows in the truth table. For each of those 2^n rows you freely choose the output to be 0 or 1, so you are making 2^n independent binary choices. The total number of ways is 2 multiplied by itself 2^n times, that is 2^(2^n).

keywords.term: Truth table of a Boolean function
keywords.explain: A complete specification: it lists the output bit for each combination of the n input variables.
keywords.example: For n = 2 the table has rows 00, 01, 10, 11, i.e. 2^2 = 4 rows.

keywords.explain: Each of the n variables is 0 or 1, so there are 2^n distinct input assignments (rows).
A Boolean function = a free assignment of one output bit (0 or 1) to each of the 2^n rows, so the count is 2 raised to the number of rows.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='538' height='248' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>Truth table (n variables)</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='160' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='30' y='66' class='sub ink'>inputs</text><text x='110' y='66' class='sub ink'>output</text><rect x='20' y='75' width='160' height='26' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='93' class='sub ink'>row 1</text><text x='118' y='93' class='sub ok'>0 or 1</text><rect x='20' y='101' width='160' height='26' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='119' class='sub ink'>row 2</text><text x='118' y='119' class='sub ok'>0 or 1</text><rect x='20' y='127' width='160' height='26' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='145' class='sub mut'>...</text><rect x='20' y='153' width='160' height='26' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='171' class='sub ink'>row 2^n</text><text x='118' y='171' class='sub ok'>0 or 1</text><text x='220' y='100' class='lbl ink'>2^n rows</text><text x='220' y='130' class='lbl ink'>2 choices each</text><text x='220' y='175' class='big ok'>2^(2^n) functions</text><text x='220' y='215' class='tag mut'>n=2 -> 2^4 = 16</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A truth table with 2^n rows where each output bit is freely 0 or 1, giving 2 raised to the 2^n rows = 2^(2^n) Boolean functions.

given.aim: Count all distinct Boolean functions on n Boolean variables.

terms.meaning: A variable taking a value in {0, 1}
terms.connects: n such variables create 2^n input combinations

terms.meaning: All assignments of 0/1 to the n variables
terms.connects: There are 2^n of them, the domain size of the function

terms.meaning: The function value (0 or 1) chosen for each input row
terms.example: Each row independently maps to 0 or 1
terms.connects: Free choice on each of the 2^n rows multiplies out

terms.meaning: A map from {0,1}^n to {0,1}, fully given by its output column
terms.example: AND, OR, XOR are particular such functions
terms.connects: Counting all output columns gives 2^(2^n)

given.plan: Note a Boolean function = a function from {0,1}^n (size 2^n) to {0,1} (size 2); apply the function-count n^m with base 2 and exponent 2^n to get 2^(2^n).

steps.title: Count the input rows
steps.apply: Each of the n Boolean variables is independently 0 or 1, so by the product rule there are 2 * 2 * ... * 2 (n times) = 2^n distinct input assignments, i.e. 2^n rows in the truth table.
steps.note: This 2^n is the size of the domain {0,1}^n of any Boolean function.

steps.title: Count output assignments over all rows
steps.apply: A Boolean function is a function from the 2^n input rows to {0, 1}. By the function-count rule, the number of functions from a set of size m = 2^n to a set of size 2 is 2^m = 2^(2^n). For example n = 2 gives 2^(2^2) = 2^4 = 16.
steps.note: Each row's output is an independent binary choice, so the total is 2 raised to the number of rows.

solution.result: The maximum number of different Boolean functions of n variables is 2^(2^n), so the answer is Option C.

questions.formula_note: A Boolean function is determined by its output on each of the 2^n input rows, so counting binary outputs over all rows gives 2^(2^n) (option C).

formulas.name: Image of a Preimage is Contained in the Set
formulas.plain: f[ f^{-1}[D] ] subseteq D  (with equality on D intersect range(f))
formulas.whenToUse: Use when composing the image map after the preimage map of a set D under a function f; the result always sits inside D.

terms.means: the preimage of D: all domain points x with f(x) in D
the forward image map applied to a set of domain points
the target subset of the codomain whose preimage is taken

formulas.trap: The reverse containment D subseteq f[f^{-1}[D]] needs f to be onto over D; in general only subseteq holds, since elements of D outside range(f) are never recovered.
formulas.reference: Standard image-preimage identities for functions in set theory

formulas.name: Unique Factorization (Prime-Power / Godel Encoding)
formulas.latex: N = \prod_{i} p_i^{e_i} \;\text{uniquely determines the exponent list }(e_1, e_2, \dots)
formulas.plain: N = prod p_i^{e_i} uniquely encodes the exponent list (e_1, e_2, ...)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to encode/decode sequences as integers: distinct exponent tuples give distinct numbers, so reading off prime exponents recovers the data exactly.

terms.means: the i-th prime number (p_1 = 2, p_2 = 3, p_3 = 5, ...)
the exponent of p_i, i.e. the i-th encoded value
the encoding integer, the product of the prime powers

formulas.trap: A number is a valid encoding only if every prime exponent is itself a legal code value; an exponent that no source can produce makes the number undecodable.
formulas.reference: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic used as a pairing/encoding function

formulas.name: Onto Composition Forces the Outer Function Onto
formulas.latex: h = f \circ g \text{ onto } \Rightarrow f \text{ onto};\quad \operatorname{range}(f\circ g) \subseteq \operatorname{range}(f)
formulas.plain: if h = f o g is onto then f is onto; range(f o g) subseteq range(f)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to deduce surjectivity facts from a composition: the last-applied (outer) function must be onto whenever the whole composition is onto.

terms.means: the outer, last-applied function whose range bounds the composition

formulas.trap: It does NOT force the inner g to be onto: g may skip elements of B as long as f still covers all of C; the dual fact is that an injective composition forces the inner function injective.
formulas.reference: Surjectivity and injectivity of function compositions

formulas.name: Double Counting of Element-Set Incidences
formulas.plain: sum_i f(i) = sum_j |X_j| = number of (i, j) with i in X_j
formulas.whenToUse: Use when a sum counts how many sets contain each element; switch to counting memberships per set, where each set contributes its size.

terms.means: the number of sets X_j that contain element i
the size of the j-th set (here every |X_j| = 3)
an incidence pair, counted once on each side

formulas.trap: Do not multiply by the number of elements m; the total is sum of set sizes, so equal-size sets give (size) times (number of sets), independent of m.
formulas.reference: Double counting / handshaking-style incidence identity in combinatorics

formulas.name: Number of Boolean Functions of n Variables
formulas.plain: number of Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n)
formulas.whenToUse: Use to count distinct functions from {0,1}^n to {0,1}: there are 2^n input rows and a free binary output per row.

terms.means: the number of Boolean input variables
the number of input combinations (truth-table rows)
free 0/1 choice on each of the 2^n rows

formulas.trap: Do not confuse 2^(2^n) with 2^n (number of input rows) or with (2^n)^? ; the exponent is the number of rows, not the number of variables.
formulas.reference: Counting functions from {0,1}^n to {0,1}; special case of n^m with n=2, m=2^n
